prompt_md: Which statement best describes **encapsulation** in object-oriented programming?

options.a: Writing all code inside a single function so nothing is exposed globally.
options.b: Bundling data and the functions that operate on it into one unit, and controlling external access.
options.c: Using structs instead of separate variables to group related data.
options.d: Making every member variable public so any function can read or write it.

explanation_md: Encapsulation means data and behavior live together *and* access is controlled — private members can only be touched by the object's own functions. Option c describes grouping (which structs already do) without the access-control component. Option d is the opposite of encapsulation.
`value` starts at 0, then `increment` adds 1 twice, so `c.value` is 2 when printed. This is the procedural style: data and logic are separate.

prompt_md: When is object-oriented programming (OOP) the **best** fit for a program?

options.a: Any time you use C++, because C++ is an OOP language.
options.b: When the program models entities with meaningful state and behavior, or has many instances of the same type.
options.c: Only when the program has more than 500 lines of code.
options.d: When you want to avoid writing functions entirely.

explanation_md: OOP shines when entities carry state and respond to operations — a `BankAccount`, a `Player`. C++ supports multiple programming styles; OOP is not mandatory. Line count is not a useful threshold, and OOP still uses functions (member functions).

prompt_md: In the OOP mindset, the difference between calling `distanceFromOrigin(p)` versus `p.distanceFromOrigin()` is:

options.a: Purely cosmetic — both styles produce identical compiled code in all cases.
options.b: The second form requires the function to be a template.
options.c: The second form makes the behavior part of the object itself, reflecting that the object knows how to compute its own distance.
options.d: The first form is faster because it avoids the overhead of an object.

explanation_md: The dot-call syntax reflects OOP's principle that behavior belongs to the data. The object isn't just a passive holder; it *does* things. Options a and d focus on performance, which is not the point of this conceptual distinction at this level.
